title: Tokens Per Second
description: The serving metric that measures how quickly a model generates output tokens once streaming has begun.
openingSummary: Tokens per second counts generated output tokens over elapsed generation time. It describes streaming pace for one answer, not queue wait, time to first token, or fleet-wide throughput across many users.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Tokens per second is the rate at which a serving system emits newly generated output tokens during an answer. The count is output tokens divided by the elapsed time spent generating those tokens after streaming begins.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Readers feel tokens per second as how fast a streamed reply arrives once it starts. Operators track it separately from time to first token, inter-token latency, and fleet throughput because it answers a different question: how quickly output tokens appear during generation.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: Imagine a chat answer that streams one hundred output tokens in five seconds after the first token appears. The tokens-per-second figure for that generation window is twenty output tokens per second.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: Tokens per second is not the same as time to first token, inter-token latency, or fleet throughput. Time to first token measures the wait before streaming starts. Inter-token latency measures the gap between individual tokens. Fleet throughput counts total output across many requests over time.

servingPath.title: Serving Path
servingPath.body: Observed tokens per second depends on decode work, cache reuse, batching, and scheduling. Follow prefill for the prompt burst before output begins, decode for the repeated next-token loop, and the prefill/decode split for how serving systems stage the two phases.
